name: Software Developer
seo_title: Career & Burnout Test for Software Developers | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Worn down by legacy code, tickets, and sprint pressure? The C.O.R.E. test shows what is really blocking you in day-to-day development work.
infobox: The reality as a software developer: You are supposed to build stable systems while requirements change in the middle of the sprint, technical debt gets ignored, and every release is under time pressure. Between incident firefighting, code reviews, ticket floods, and meetings, there is barely any room for focused work. At the same time, you are expected to master new tools, frameworks, and architectures all the time. That creates constant tension: functional on the outside, exhausted and irritated on the inside, often with the feeling that you are never really done.
seo_deepdive_h2: The core conflict: high quality standards in broken delivery systems
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like "prioritize better" or "set clearer boundaries" often falls short for developers. If your calendar is full of context switching, Product keeps delivering unclear requirements, and legacy systems blow up every estimate, the problem is not just your time management. Many developers still stay quiet out of fear of being seen as uncooperative, slow, or technically weak. That is exactly where imposter syndrome, conflict avoidance, and existential fear kick in. The C.O.R.E. approach cleanly separates real system overload from your unconscious patterns that keep you stuck in it.
faq_q1: What do I get out of the test if my main problem is a chaotic team and bad product management?
faq_a1: That is exactly why the diagnosis matters. It does not claim your stress only comes from inside you. It checks how strongly external factors like unclear priorities, constant context switching, missing technical leadership, or unrealistic deadlines are driving your situation. At the same time, it shows whether fear of conflict, pressure for approval, or weak boundaries make you systematically overcompensate. That way you can see whether you mainly have a structural problem, a role problem, or a psychological attachment pattern.

name: System Administrator
seo_title: Burnout & Career Test for System Administrators | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Constant fire from tickets, on-call duty, and blame when things break? The C.O.R.E. test shows what really wears you down in sysadmin work.
infobox: The reality as a system administrator: You keep infrastructure running that almost nobody notices until something breaks. Then you suddenly become the escalation point for everything: AD problems, backup failures, VPN outages, permissions, patching, printers, mail, on-call. Many environments suffer from technical sprawl, poor documentation, and chronic underinvestment. At the same time, people expect zero outages, maximum security, and instant response. The job quickly turns into constant tension because your success is invisible, but your mistakes are visible to everyone.
seo_deepdive_h2: The core conflict: invisible responsibility under permanent escalation
seo_deepdive_text: System administrators are often told they just need to prioritize better or set clearer boundaries. That misses the point. If you are partly responsible for critical systems, outdated infrastructure, and missing redundancy, tension is not a personal time-management issue but a logical result of the setup. Many sysadmins compensate with control issues, constant availability, or silent extra work. Behind that are often existential fear after outages, a helper complex in support, and fear of being seen as incompetent. The C.O.R.E. Index cleanly separates real system failure from your own psychological attachment to the role.
faq_q1: What do I get out of the test if my main problem is a chaotic IT landscape with legacy systems and constant on-call duty?
faq_a1: That is exactly where the analysis starts. It does not just assess your stress level, but the interaction between technical legacy issues, organizational understaffing, and your personal response pattern. That makes it visible whether bad structures are mainly wearing you down or whether weak boundaries, exaggerated responsibility, and fear of escalation are causing you to carry too much for too long.

name: Data Analyst
seo_title: Career & Burnout Test: Data Analyst | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Tired of data chaos, ad-hoc requests, and KPI politics? The C.O.R.E. test shows what really blocks you as a data analyst.
infobox: The reality as a data analyst: You are supposed to derive clean decisions from fragmented data, even though tracking is incomplete, definitions keep changing, and stakeholders wanted results yesterday. A big part of your work is not analysis, but data cleaning, ticket ping-pong, and defending numbers against gut feeling and internal politics. At the same time, you are expected to be precise even though data models, ownership, and priorities in the company are often chaotic. That is where the constant stress comes from: high responsibility, low control, and permanent context switching.
seo_deepdive_h2: The core conflict: delivering precision in a system that rewards fuzziness
seo_deepdive_text: Standard tips like "prioritize better" or "communicate more clearly" fall short here. As a data analyst, you often sit between business, engineering, and management and are supposed to smooth out contradictions that are structurally created. Many stay stuck in this role too long because they want recognition through performance, avoid conflict with stakeholders, or triple-check everything out of fear of mistakes. That creates a toxic mix of imposter syndrome, weak boundaries, and silent existential fear: you function, but you are no longer moving forward cleanly in your career.
faq_q1: What do I get out of the test if my main problem is poor data quality and changing KPI definitions?
faq_a1: That is exactly where the diagnosis starts. It does not just assess your stress level, but the psychological impact of systemic data problems on your behavior: withdrawal, perfectionism, conflict avoidance, or over-adaptation. That makes it visible whether the company with its missing data governance is mainly wearing you down, or whether internal patterns are causing you to carry unclear requirements for too long instead of setting boundaries or demanding proper escalation.

name: Data Scientist
seo_title: Career & Burnout Test for Data Scientists | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Tired of stakeholder politics, data chaos, and model pressure? The C.O.R.E. test shows what really blocks you as a data scientist.
infobox: The reality as a data scientist: You are paid for statistical precision, but you often work with broken data, conflicting business goals, and politically colored expectations. Between ad-hoc analyses, questionable KPI definitions, and pressure to build reliable models from dirty datasets, there is little room for clean methodology. On top of that comes the constant need to justify yourself: Why does feature engineering take so long, why is the model not in production, why does the prediction not deliver miracles? Many data scientists get stuck between research ideals, product reality, and management delusions.
seo_deepdive_h2: When analytical integrity meets business theater
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like "prioritize better" or "communicate more clearly" falls short here. The core problem is often not time management, but a permanent conflict between scientific standards and an organization that wants quick, simple answers. Many data scientists compensate for this pressure with over-preparation, perfectionism, or silent adaptation. Behind that are often imposter syndrome, conflict avoidance, and the fear of being seen as expendable without visible business impact. The C.O.R.E. Index cleanly separates whether dysfunctional structures are mainly slowing you down or whether unconscious status and security fears are sabotaging your boundaries.
faq_q1: What do I get out of the test if my main problem is bad data and unrealistic stakeholder expectations?
faq_a1: That is exactly where the diagnosis starts. It does not claim your problem is just "mindset." It checks how strongly systemic factors like data quality, unclear ownership, KPI politics, and delivery pressure are driving your exhaustion — and whether perfectionism, pressure for approval, or fear of conflict are making you stay too long in dysfunctional setups.

name: IT Support Employee
seo_title: Burnout Test for IT Support Employees | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Tickets, escalations, constant pressure? The C.O.R.E. test shows whether system failures, the helper role, or weak boundaries are wearing you down in IT support.
infobox: The reality as an IT support employee: You are the first buffer for frustration, outages, and technical cluelessness. Your day is made up of ticket backlogs, priority chaos, SLA pressure, and users who report too late and then expect instant fixes. On top of that, you are supposed to stay friendly while dealing with the consequences of bad documentation, broken processes, and half-baked rollouts. People in support often carry responsibility without real decision-making power — and that is what wears you down over time.
seo_deepdive_h2: When constant availability becomes a zone of psychological wear
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like "set boundaries" often falls short in IT support. If inboxes are overflowing, P1 tickets are escalating, and every interruption is immediately visible, boundaries are not just a matter of discipline, but of the system. Many support employees get trapped in a helper role where they secure recognition through constant availability. Add fear of mistakes, conflict avoidance toward business teams, and the feeling of never knowing enough. The C.O.R.E. Index shows whether the job is really blocking you — or your unconscious attachment to this constant strain.
faq_q1: What do I get out of the test if my main problem is constant escalations and unrealistic SLAs?
faq_a1: It cleanly separates structural pressure from your individual response pattern. If your environment is objectively dysfunctional, the test makes that visible. At the same time, it shows whether you are making escalations worse because you delegate poorly, avoid conflict, try to solve everything yourself, or tie your self-worth to fast problem solving.

name: DevOps Engineer
seo_title: Burnout Test for DevOps Engineers | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: On-call, incident pressure, endless deployments? The C.O.R.E. test shows whether system chaos, constant stress, or weak boundaries are wearing you down.
infobox: The reality as a DevOps engineer: You are supposed to deliver stability, speed, and security at the same time, usually with too little time, half-finished architecture, and conflicting goals. By day you build CI/CD pipelines, by night incidents escalate because monitoring, ownership, or runbooks were never properly clarified. You are stuck between developers, security, platform, and management, responsible for uptime but often without real decision-making power. The price: permanent alarm state, context switching, and the feeling that one mistake will immediately get expensive.
seo_deepdive_h2: When permanent operational responsibility eats every boundary
seo_deepdive_text: DevOps rarely fails because of missing articles or one more Kubernetes course. The core problem is often psychological and structural: you become the silent dumping ground for bad processes, unclear responsibilities, and unrealistic release expectations. Many stay in this role because fear of failure, imposter syndrome, or a technical helper complex keeps them from setting clear boundaries. Standard tips like 'prioritize' or 'communicate better' fall short when your nervous system is already in permanent alarm and you catch every escalation personally.
faq_q1: What do I get out of the test if my main problem is constant on-call load and incident fatigue?
faq_a1: It cleanly separates systemic overload from your individual response pattern. Specifically, the evaluation shows whether bad operational structures, weak boundaries, exaggerated responsibility, or hidden existential fear are keeping you stuck in the on-call loop. That matters because 'more resilience' does not solve the problem if pagers, ownership, and escalation paths in the company are fundamentally broken.

name: UX/UI Designer
seo_title: Career & Burnout Test for UX/UI Designers | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Worn down by stakeholder feedback, design by committee, and endless iterations? C.O.R.E. shows what really blocks you in UX/UI work.
infobox: The reality as a UX/UI designer: You are supposed to work user-centered, but often deliver under roadmap pressure, half-baked requirements, and contradictory stakeholder feedback. Between research, wireframes, UI systems, tickets, and fast handoffs, proper UX often gets left behind. Design by committee tears apart clear concepts while product, sales, and management sell their personal opinions as user truth. At the same time, you have to justify every decision even though there is often simply no time for real validation, usability tests, or proper iteration.
seo_deepdive_h2: The core conflict: delivering empathy in a system optimized for output
seo_deepdive_text: UX/UI design rarely fails because of missing Figma skills. The problem is usually a system that praises research but wants screens. Standard advice like 'communicate more clearly' falls short when you are being worn down between PM, dev, brand, and HiPPOs. Many designers compensate with perfectionism, conflict avoidance, or excessive adaptation. Behind that are often imposter syndrome, fear of visibility, and the worry of being dismissed as 'just visual.' Those exact patterns keep you stuck in loops of justification, rework, and silent exhaustion.
faq_q1: What if my biggest stress is not the design work itself, but constant stakeholder reviews without clear decision logic?
faq_a1: Then the burden is very likely not primarily in your professional role, but in a dysfunctional decision environment. That is exactly what the diagnosis separates: professional overload from political friction, weak boundaries, and chronic external control. If every opinion counts as much as research, constant tension is the result, because you carry responsibility but have almost no real authority. The test makes visible whether you are mainly suffering from bad governance or whether internal patterns like fear of conflict and a need for harmony are making the situation worse.

name: Project Manager
seo_title: Burnout & Career Test for Project Managers | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Caught between scope creep, stakeholder pressure, and constant escalation? The C.O.R.E. test shows whether system failures or weak boundaries are wearing you down.
infobox: The reality as a project manager: You carry responsibility for deadlines, budget, and delivery, often without real authority. Between scope creep, shifting priorities, and politically charged stakeholder rounds, you are supposed to create commitment even though prerequisites, resources, and decisions keep falling apart. You moderate conflicts, absorb escalations, and stay calm on the outside while dependencies break internally. The real stress comes not only from workload, but from permanent liability for results you can only partly control.
seo_deepdive_h2: When responsibility is higher than your real influence
seo_deepdive_text: Project managers are often told to prioritize more cleanly, escalate harder, or bring stakeholders along better. That falls short when you are structurally being worn down between line organization, business units, and management. Many stay in this role under constant stress because fear of conflict, a need for harmony, or a hidden helper complex keeps them from setting boundaries early. On top of that comes imposter pressure: if you do not look confident in every meeting, you fear losing authority immediately. The C.O.R.E. Index soberly separates what is a dysfunctional setup from what your unconscious patterns are locking into place.
faq_q1: What if my project keeps escalating even though I plan and communicate properly?
faq_a1: Then the problem is often not your method, but a structural mismatch of unrealistic promises, missing decision clarity, and conflicting stakeholder interests. Good planning does not compensate for an organization that changes priorities daily or buries risks politically. The relevant question is therefore not only what you can manage better, but why you are taking responsibility for conditions that are outside your influence. That is exactly the boundary the test makes visible.

name: Managing Director
seo_title: Burnout & Career Test for Managing Directors | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Pressure from advisory board, bank, and workforce? The C.O.R.E. test shows whether system load, isolation, or fear of losing control is blocking you.
infobox: The reality as a managing director: You carry overall responsibility, but real relief is rare. Between liquidity planning, personnel issues, shareholder pressure, liability risks, and operational firefighting, there is hardly any room for clear strategy. Outwardly you are supposed to project confidence, internally you are supposed to speed up decisions, and at the same time catch every escalation. Many managing directors live in permanent alarm mode: too much responsibility, too little honest feedback, and an environment where weakness is immediately read as a leadership problem.
seo_deepdive_h2: Isolation at the top: when responsibility turns into constant tension
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like delegating, resilience training, or better time management often falls short here. The core problem is usually not technique, but psychodynamics: the fear of giving up control, the worry about losing face with shareholders, or the vague feeling of being replaceable at any time. Many managing directors compensate for this tension with over-responsibility, micromanagement, or constant availability. The C.O.R.E. Index soberly checks whether real system constraints are wearing you down or whether unconscious existential fear, imposter pressure, and weak boundaries are making the situation worse.
faq_q1: How does the test help me if, as a managing director, I am constantly under pressure between shareholders, the leadership team, and the bank?
faq_a1: It cleanly separates external system pressure from your individual response pattern. That makes it visible whether the company situation, role conflicts, and liability proximity are the main burden, or whether internal drivers like control issues, conflict avoidance, or status fear are causing you to carry too much yourself and delay critical decisions for too long.

name: Office Manager
seo_title: Burnout & Career Test for Office Managers | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Caught between chaos, ad-hoc tasks, and missing recognition? The C.O.R.E. test shows whether system failures or weak boundaries are burning you out.
infobox: The reality as an office manager: You keep the place together in the background, but that is exactly why almost nobody sees what you actually do. Calendar chaos, travel bookings, invoices, orders, onboarding, spontaneous boss requests, and the team’s emotional waste disposal all land on you at once. Your work is critical, but often invisible as long as nothing escalates. Many office managers work in permanent reaction mode, absorb the company’s structural deficits, and then get reduced to 'organizational talent' instead of being taken seriously as an operational hub.
seo_deepdive_h2: When helpfulness turns into constant availability
seo_deepdive_text: Standard tips like setting priorities or communicating more clearly often fall short here. As an office manager, you are not just responsible for tasks, but for the friction in the whole system. People who function heavily through adaptation, duty, and conflict avoidance quickly become the silent backup plan for everyone else. That is where professionalism turns into self-overload. The C.O.R.E. Index checks whether chaotic structures are mainly wearing you down or whether unconscious patterns like a helper complex, need for approval, imposter syndrome, or fear of rejection are stopping you from enforcing boundaries effectively.
faq_q1: What can I do if management keeps throwing ad-hoc tasks at me and my actual work keeps getting pushed aside?
faq_a1: That is usually not a pure time-management problem, but a role and prioritization problem. If everything lands on you as urgent, the company often lacks a clean decision cascade. You need visible priorities, documented responsibilities, and clear feedback to your managers about which tasks are being displaced by new demands. The test helps you see whether you are mainly suffering from bad organization or whether your own conflict avoidance and constant availability are unintentionally stabilizing the pattern.

name: HR Officer
seo_title: Burnout & Career Test for HR Officers | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Crushed between departments, labor law, and staff shortages? The C.O.R.E. test shows what is really blocking you in day-to-day HR work.
infobox: The reality as an HR officer: You sit at the intersection of the workforce, managers, and executive leadership — and take pressure from every side. On one hand, you're supposed to retain employees, resolve conflicts, and stay compliant with labor law; on the other, cut costs, fill vacancies fast, and implement unpopular decisions. Add warnings, termination talks, recruiting bottlenecks, works council issues, and managers who only involve HR once things have already blown up. The result: heavy emotional load, constant availability, and almost no room for clean boundaries.
seo_deepdive_h2: The core conflict in HR: wanting to help, but having to deliver hard lines
seo_deepdive_text: Generic advice like "prioritize better" often falls flat in HR. As an HR officer, you carry responsibility for people and at the same time for rules, risks, and company interests. That role conflict is exactly what makes the job psychologically draining. Anyone strongly driven by harmony, recognition, or duty tends to stay available too long, absorb leadership failures, and take on the system's emotional leftovers. The C.O.R.E. Index shows whether conflict fear, a helper complex, weak boundaries, or fear of losing professional relevance are keeping you stuck in an exhausting HR role.
faq_q1: What can I do when managers only involve HR once employee conflicts have already escalated and I'm expected to limit the damage?
faq_a1: That's a classic structural problem, not automatically a personal flaw. From a professional standpoint, it makes sense to define escalation paths, responsibilities, and documentation standards clearly instead of repeatedly rescuing what others failed to handle. Psychologically, the key question is whether you keep taking on the firefighter role because of conflict avoidance or a need for approval. That's exactly where the diagnostics start: they separate systemic mismanagement from your individual pattern of taking on too much responsibility for other people's leadership failures.

name: Accountant
seo_title: Burnout & Career Test for Accountants | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Constant stress from closings, deadlines, and fear of mistakes? The C.O.R.E. test shows whether system pressure or inner blocks are wearing you down.
infobox: The reality as an accountant: You work in a job where small mistakes get expensive fast and almost never stay hidden. Month-end close, year-end close, VAT returns, account reconciliations, open items, and follow-up questions from tax advisors, management, or auditors rarely happen neatly one after another — they happen all at once. Add the contradiction of maximum accuracy and permanent time pressure. People in accounting carry responsibility without much recognition: as long as everything balances, nobody notices. The moment one number is off, you're immediately in the spotlight.
seo_deepdive_h2: When fear of mistakes and constant responsibility eat up every boundary
seo_deepdive_text: Accountants are often told to set priorities and draw cleaner boundaries. That misses the point, because your day is not made up of arbitrary to-dos, but of deadlines, liability pressure, and auditable numbers. Many stay in a state of constant tension: afraid of missing a mistake with financial consequences, not being technically good enough, or seeming uncooperative. Especially conscientious people get trapped in overcontrol, silent overtime, and a permanent state of alert. The C.O.R.E. Index clearly separates what is system pressure from what comes from unconscious existential fear or weak boundaries.
faq_q1: What if month-end close and the fear of booking errors are what wear me down most?
faq_a1: Then the problem is often not just workload, but a mix of high sensitivity to error costs, constant interruptions, and no psychological decompression after critical phases. The assessment specifically checks whether your strain comes mainly from real process chaos, unclear responsibilities, and deadline pressure — or whether exaggerated perfectionism, conflict avoidance, and constant self-monitoring are making the stress worse.

name: Team Leader
seo_title: Burnout & Career Test for Team Leaders | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Caught between pressure from above and conflict in the team? The C.O.R.E. test shows whether your role, boundaries, or inner patterns are blocking you as a team leader.
infobox: The reality as a team leader: You're permanently stuck between fronts: target specs, cost-cutting rounds, and reporting pressure above; conflict, overload, and expectations of backup below. You're supposed to deliver technically, absorb the human side, jump in operationally, and still secure performance. Many team leaders get promoted without ever being properly prepared for leadership. The result: escalations, hidden loyalty conflicts, endless coordination, and the feeling that you personally have to take the blame for the system's failures.
seo_deepdive_h2: The core conflict: responsibility without real control
seo_deepdive_text: Generic advice like "delegate clearly" or "prioritize better" falls apart when you're formally responsible but have neither enough staff nor real decision-making power. That's where leadership often turns into chronic tension. Many team leaders compensate for structural deficits with extra work because conflict fear, a helper complex, or fear of losing authority keeps them from setting boundaries. Others slide into imposter syndrome because they came from a specialist role and are suddenly expected to lead people instead of tasks. The C.O.R.E. Index separates it cleanly: what is system failure, and what is your inner pattern?
faq_q1: What can I do if, as a team leader, I'm constantly plugging operational gaps and therefore have no time to actually lead?
faq_a1: That's a classic sign of role blur. If you're constantly doing the work yourself, you may keep operations afloat in the short term, but in the medium term you undermine leadership, development, and accountability in the team. The first step is not more efficiency, but a clean diagnosis: Which tasks are real leadership work, and which ones are just compensating for staff shortages, bad processes, or lack of clarity from above? Only once that separation is visible can delegation, escalation, and boundaries be built in a way that actually holds.

name: Controller
seo_title: Career & Burnout Test for Controllers | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Crushed between forecast pressure, month-end close, and management politics? The C.O.R.E. test shows what is really blocking you in controlling.
infobox: The reality as a controller: You're supposed to deliver numbers that hold up, even though data sources are shaky, responsibilities are fuzzy, and deadlines are non-negotiable. Between month-end close, forecast, budget rounds, and ad hoc analyses, you're often the person who makes contradictions visible — and gets disliked for it. Departments want flexibility, management wants predictability, and you're stuck in the middle. The constant pressure to deliver precision under time constraints fuels perfectionism, overcontrol, and a chronic state of alert.

seo_deepdive_text: Controllers are often told to set priorities and communicate clearly. That misses the point when your entire job is based on spotting errors early, naming risks, and saying uncomfortable truths. Many stay stuck in overload loops because unconscious existential fear, perfectionism, or conflict avoidance keeps them from pushing back on vague assignments or questioning politically charged numbers. The C.O.R.E. Index separates systemic pressure from your inner patterns: blunt, psychologically grounded, and without motivational fluff.
faq_q1: What does the test give me if my main problem is the constant stream of ad hoc requests right before reporting?
faq_a1: It shows whether you're mainly being worn down by poor management, missing processes, and political escalation patterns — or whether you yourself are taking on too much because of fear of mistakes, rejection, or losing control. That distinction is crucial in controlling, because not every overload problem is a time-management issue; often it's a mix of system failures and weak boundaries.

name: Early Childhood Educator
seo_title: Burnout & Career Test for Early Childhood Educators | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: At the limit between staff shortages, noise, and pressure from parents? The C.O.R.E. test shows whether system stress or weak boundaries are wearing you down.
infobox: The reality as an early childhood educator: Every day you carry responsibility for attachment, safety, development, and documentation — usually all at once and under chronic staff shortages. While children need emotional co-regulation, providers, managers, and parents expect smooth operations, development reports, and complete observation records. Breaks get canceled, the noise level stays high, and team absences become normal. Anyone working in this job learns fast: professional skill alone does not protect you from overload when a tight system runs on your patience, your sense of duty, and your constant availability.

seo_deepdive_text: Early childhood educators are often told to set better boundaries or improve time management. That misses the point. In daycare centers and residential groups, boundary-setting often fails not because of lack of knowledge, but because of guilt: if you call in sick, colleagues carry more, children lose stability, and parents react irritably. That's exactly where professional commitment easily turns into a helper complex. Add conflict avoidance, team loyalty pressure, and fear of being seen as not resilient. The C.O.R.E. Index cleanly separates systemic overload from unconscious patterns that keep you in constant stress despite the warning signs.
faq_q1: How do I tell whether my exhaustion comes from the facility or from my inability to set boundaries with the team and parents?
faq_a1: That distinction is central. The test doesn't just measure how stressed you feel; it looks at the cause structure behind it: staffing ratios, absence culture, noise, documentation pressure, and role conflicts on one side; conflict avoidance, exaggerated responsibility, and guilt around boundaries on the other. That makes it visible whether you're mainly working in a chronically dysfunctional system or whether inner patterns are causing you to silently absorb extra load over and over.

name: Teacher
seo_title: Burnout & Career Test for Teachers | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Exhausted by grading, parent work, and constant noise? C.O.R.E. shows whether system pressure, a helper complex, or weak boundaries are wearing you down.
infobox: The reality as a teacher: You don't just teach subjects — you hold together a fragile system every day: overcrowded classes, inclusion without resources, behavioral issues, pressure from parents, piles of grading, meetings, and documentation duties. Officially it's about education; in practice it's often crisis management and relationship work under time pressure. At the same time, you're expected to stay composed, fair, and emotionally stable even when noise, discipline problems, and constant availability are systematically destroying your concentration and recovery.

seo_deepdive_text: Teachers are often told to prioritize better or set clearer boundaries. That misses the point, because school is not a normal office job: when you set boundaries, the conflict often lands directly with students, parents, or colleagues. That's exactly where unconscious patterns hit hardest — helper syndrome, guilt, fear of losing authority, or the compulsion to absorb everything. The C.O.R.E. Index analyzes whether external overload is wearing you down, or whether inner drivers are keeping you trapped in extra work, guilt, and exhaustion.
faq_q1: How does the test distinguish normal strain in school life from a real burnout risk?
faq_a1: It doesn't just assess your stress level, but the cause of the chronic strain: for example, weak boundaries with parents, moral pressure toward students, conflict avoidance among colleagues, or the compulsion to be permanently perfect in teaching and grading. That makes it visible whether you're mainly suffering from systemic problems or whether inner patterns are making the overload worse.

name: Social Worker
seo_title: Burnout & Career Test for Social Workers | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Crushed between caseloads, crises, and bureaucracy? The C.O.R.E. test shows whether system pressure or weak boundaries are blocking you.

That creates massive internal pressure: you know professionally what clients need, but because of responsibilities, waiting lists, and institutional limits, you can't implement it consistently. That helplessness eats deep into motivation and mental health over time.

seo_deepdive_text: Generic advice like better time management or more self-care often falls short in social work. The core problem is not just stress, but moral overload: you see hardship directly, yet have to act within legal, financial, and institutional limits. Many social workers compensate with over-responsibility, poor boundaries, and fear of letting clients down. Add guilt when cases escalate or help isn't enough. The C.O.R.E. Index separates it cleanly: what is systemic wear and tear, and what is your own pattern of helper syndrome, conflict avoidance, or quiet exhaustion?
faq_q1: How does the test help me if I can't switch off after risk assessments or crisis cases?
faq_a1: The test doesn't replace supervision or trauma therapy, but it shows you precisely which psychological mechanisms kick in after stressful cases. It makes visible whether you're mainly exhausted by secondary traumatization, chronic over-responsibility, weak boundaries, or a dysfunctional work environment. name: Special Education Teacher
seo_title: Burnout Test for Special Education Teachers | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Crushed between support plans, inclusion, and staff shortages? The C.O.R.E. test shows whether system pressure or weak boundaries are burning you out.
infobox: The reality as a special education teacher: You work in a field that officially talks about participation, but in practice is chronically understaffed. Between diagnostic assessments, IEP/support plans, parent meetings, inclusion demands, and acute crisis intervention, there's barely any room for proper educational work. You're supposed to provide individualized support even though time, space, and multidisciplinary coordination are missing. At the same time, you carry the emotional weight of behavioral issues, escalations, and quiet setbacks — and often become the buffer for a system that sells shortage management as support.
seo_deepdive_h2: When helping turns into self-sacrifice
seo_deepdive_text: Generic advice like "set better boundaries" falls short here, because special education is built on moral responsibility. If you don't step in, a child, a class, or an overloaded team may be left hanging. That's exactly what makes the job vulnerable to helper syndrome, guilt, and chronic boundary violations. Add the fear of never being good enough professionally: diagnostics, parent work, inclusion, documentation, case conferences. The C.O.R.E. Index checks whether it's mainly the broken system exhausting you, or whether unconscious conflict fear and self-overload are blocking your way out of overload.
faq_q1: What if I'm at my limit not because of the children, but because of documentation, inclusion pressure, and missing resources?
faq_a1: That's a typical pattern in special education. The strain often doesn't come primarily from relationship work with students, but from contradictory system demands: individualized support without time, legally sound documentation without relief, inclusion without enough staff. The assessment therefore separates emotional exhaustion from casework very precisely from structural overload caused by the framework conditions. That makes it visible whether you're mainly suffering from the job itself — or from the conditions you're expected to work under.

name: School Support Assistant
seo_title: Burnout & Suitability Test for School Support Assistants | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Crushed between child, teacher, and parents? The C.O.R.E. test shows whether system pressure, a helper complex, or weak boundaries are wearing you down.
infobox: The reality as a school support assistant: You're supposed to give one child stability, but you work in a system that is chronically overloaded. Often you're dropped into inclusion classes without proper onboarding, expected to absorb escalations, set boundaries, and stay invisible at the same time. Teachers expect relief, parents expect safety, providers expect functioning. Professionally, you carry a lot of responsibility; formally, you often have surprisingly little influence. That imbalance eats away at self-worth, nerves, and professional identity over time.
seo_deepdive_h2: When responsibility is high but authority is missing
seo_deepdive_text: Generic tips like "set better boundaries" often fall short here. As a school support assistant, you're in the middle of a triangle between child, school, and home, without real decision-making power. Many stay in overwhelming assignments too long because helper syndrome, conflict fear, or fear of losing the job are stronger than self-protection. Add the quiet self-devaluation: when a child escalates or integration fails, you quickly blame yourself. The C.O.R.E. Index cleanly separates what is systemic failure from where unconscious patterns keep you in a wear-and-tear mode.
faq_q1: What can I do if I'm constantly blamed for everything in class, but I get no say in support goals or rules?
faq_a1: That's a classic role conflict in school support work. The key is to define your role professionally and clearly: support, observation, de-escalation, and feedback yes — overall educational responsibility and school policy decisions no. Document recurring situations concretely, insist on case meetings with the teacher and provider, and get responsibilities clarified in writing. If you're expected to carry liability without influence, that's not a personal flaw — it's a structural problem that has to be limited professionally.

name: Special Needs Care Worker

faq_a1: The test doesn't just assess whether your day is exhausting; it looks at the patterns behind it: constantly stepping in, guilt when setting boundaries, emotional exhaustion after crisis shifts, cynicism, inner distance, or physical warning signs. name: Early Childhood Educator

The test makes visible whether you are mainly suffering from bad working conditions or whether unconscious patterns are causing you to ignore warning signs, take on extra load, and get stuck professionally.

name: Key Account Manager
seo_title: Career & Stress Test for Key Account Managers | C.O.R.E.

You are at the same time relationship manager, escalation buffer, and internal lobbyist. seo_deepdive_text: In key account management, people often say you just need to negotiate more resiliently and set clearer boundaries. That is too simplistic. If you depend on a few major customers, loss anxiety builds fast: every conflict feels like a risk to revenue, bonus, and internal status. That is where over-adaptation, constant availability, and false concessions come from. Standard sales tips do not help when unconscious existential fear, a helper complex, or the need for recognition make you blur red lines. The C.O.R.E. Index cleanly separates systemic sales pressure from your own psychological patterns.
faq_q1: What does the test do for me if my main problem is internal escalation between customer, sales, operations, and pricing?
faq_a1: That is exactly where the analysis starts. In key account management, the burden often does not sit in the sales conversation, but in the sandwich position between external demands and internal goal conflicts. The test checks whether dysfunctional structures are mainly grinding you down, or whether conflict avoidance, the need for approval, and weak boundaries are causing you to overcompensate instead of managing properly.

name: Sales Representative
seo_title: Career & Burnout Test for Sales | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Suffering from rejections, KPI pressure, and forecast theater? The C.O.R.E. test shows whether system failures or inner blocks are grinding down your sales performance.
infobox: The reality as a sales representative: You live under constant target and forecast pressure. Pipeline, closing rate, objection handling, follow-up, CRM maintenance, and reporting all run in parallel while customers reply late, freeze budgets, or ghost you after the third meeting. At the same time, you are expected to look confident on the outside, even when the end-of-month push is already taking a toll. Many people in sales are stuck between commission pressure, constant rejection, and the expectation to start from zero again every week.
seo_deepdive_h2: When closing pressure meets fear of rejection
seo_deepdive_text: In sales, the standard advice is often: make more calls, handle objections better, close harder. That is too narrow. If you are internally dependent on approval, avoid conflict, or take rejections personally, you sabotage yourself before the pitch even starts. Then you discount too early, talk too much, ask for the close too late, or keep chasing every silent customer. At the same time, unrealistic targets and politically polished forecasts create chronic stress. The C.O.R.E. Index cleanly separates what is making you sick in your environment from where existential fear, people-pleasing, or self-doubt are blocking your performance.
faq_q1: Why do I cave in during sales conversations on price and give discounts too early?
faq_a1: Most of the time this is not just a negotiation issue, but a psychological pattern. If you strongly want to avoid rejection, price resistance quickly feels like personal dismissal, and you try to remove the tension immediately with a discount. Short term, that lowers pressure. Long term, it destroys margin, authority, and self-confidence. What matters is not only sales technique, but whether fear of conflict, closing pressure, or a weak internal standard is controlling your behavior.

name: Account Manager
seo_title: Burnout & Career Test for Account Managers | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Caught between revenue pressure, churn risk, and escalations? The C.O.R.E. test shows whether system failures or weak boundaries are grinding you down in account management.
infobox: The reality as an account manager: You carry revenue responsibility, are supposed to retain existing customers, place cross-sells, and absorb every escalation at the same time. In many companies, you are the buffer zone between impatient customers, overwhelmed delivery teams, and unrealistic forecasts from management. You have to sell reliability even though internal processes are shaky. The constant pressure to prevent churn and still deliver growth creates permanent tension. If you do not set clear boundaries, you quickly end up in a role where you are responsible for everything but have almost no real control.
seo_deepdive_h2: The core conflict: responsibility without real control
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like "prioritize better" or "communicate more clearly" often falls short in account management. The problem is structural: you are supposed to secure customer loyalty even though product gaps, pricing stress, or internal SLA breaches are not in your hands. Many account managers compensate with over-responsibility, constant availability, and false loyalty to the customer. Behind that are often loss anxiety, fear of conflict, or the need to be indispensable. These patterns make you a target for escalations, destroy your boundaries, and keep you in a work mode that looks professional from the outside but drains you internally.
faq_q1: What can I do if I am blamed for churn even though product and service disappoint the customer?
faq_a1: First: not every cancellation is a sales failure. In account management, responsibility is often pushed toward the person who is visible to the customer. The key is to depersonalize churn properly: with solid data on usage, support cases, time to resolution, price development, and expectation management in the sales handover. If you do not document structural causes, you will quickly become the scapegoat. Psychologically, it gets dangerous when you start compensating for systemic deficits with extra work and permanent availability. That is exactly where solid diagnostics come in.

name: Electrician

Index checks whether existential fear, conflict avoidance toward customers, or exaggerated perfectionism are what keep you from acting more clearly.

Index cleanly separates whether the environment is really burning you out or whether imposter syndrome, conflict avoidance, and weak boundaries are making things worse.
