seo_deepdive_h2: When measurability turns into constant tension
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like 'prioritize better' or 'communicate more clearly' falls short here. In online marketing, you are tied to platforms, algorithms, and data you never fully control. That creates a deep form of chronic stress: when results fluctuate, you first blame yourself. From that come overcontrol, constant tweaking, and the inability to mentally switch off. The C.O.R.E. Index checks whether real system problems are wearing you down or whether fear of failure, imposter syndrome, and weak boundaries are trapping you in an endless optimization mode.
faq_q1: What good is the test if my main problem is bad attribution and unrealistic ROAS targets?
faq_a1: It separates systemic causes from your psychological reaction pattern. If tracking setups, consent losses, or management fantasies are the core problem, the evaluation makes that clear. At the same time, it shows whether you take on too much responsibility out of fear of criticism, personalize every fluctuation, and end up in an unhealthy compulsion to control.

name: Journalist
seo_title: Burnout & Career Test for Journalists | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Crushed between click pressure, deadlines, and precarious pay? The C.O.R.E. test shows what is really blocking you in journalism.
infobox: The reality as a Journalist: You work under constant time pressure, often with thin facts, tight newsroom budgets, and a market that values reach over care. Between newsdesk, copy desk criticism, SEO requirements, and social media analysis, there is little room for proper research. Freelancers fight dumping fees, permanent staff face layoffs and work intensification. On top of that comes the psychological wear from crisis topics, hate messages, and the constant fear of making a professional or moral mistake in public.
seo_deepdive_h2: When truth claims meet click logic and self-doubt
seo_deepdive_text: Standard tips like better time management or more resilience often fall short in journalism. The problem is not just the deadline, but the permanent conflict between editorial standards and commercial pressure. Many journalists stay in overloaded structures because fear of survival, the need for recognition, or an exaggerated sense of responsibility sabotages any boundary-setting. Anyone who constantly has to prove they are relevant, resilient, and untouchable quickly slips into hypervigilance, self-censorship, or exhaustion. The C.O.R.E. Index makes these unconscious patterns visible and separates personal blockages from systemic newsroom stress.
faq_q1: Will the test help me if, as a freelance journalist, I keep taking assignments even though the fees are too low?
faq_a1: Yes. That is often not just a pricing problem, but a psychological pattern made up of fear of survival, conflict avoidance, and external validation. The evaluation shows whether you are acting out of real market pressure or whether unconscious fear of loss is stopping you from setting boundaries, renegotiating fees, or consistently rejecting unprofitable assignments.

name: Video Editor
seo_title: Career & Burnout Test for Video Editors | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Worn down by deadlines, feedback loops, and price pressure? The C.O.R.E. test shows what is really blocking you as a video editor.
infobox: The reality as a Video Editor: You rarely just sit down and cut. You manage chaotic briefs, missing or bad footage, contradictory client feedback, and deadlines that have nothing to do with proper post-production. At the same time, every cut is supposed to feel 'creative', even though budget, timing, and approval processes squeeze you into a narrow corridor. Then come endless review loops, pixel-level nitpicking over trivialities, and the constant devaluation of your work with lines like 'It is just a small edit.'
seo_deepdive_h2: When perfectionism and approval chaos grind you down systematically
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like 'prioritize better' often misses the point in editing, because the problem is rarely just your time management. You work in a system of subjective taste, unclear responsibilities, and constant last-minute changes. Many video editors compensate for this chaos with exaggerated perfectionism, silent overtime, and conflict avoidance. Behind that are often fear of survival, imposter syndrome, and the hope that adapting will finally get them recognized as professionals. Exactly these patterns keep you stuck in bad processes, dumping fees, or chronic exhaustion.
faq_q1: Does the test help if endless revision loops with clients or creative directors are wearing me out?
faq_a1: Yes. That is exactly where the analysis starts. It cleanly separates external process failure, such as bad briefing, missing approval structure, or scope creep, from your own patterns like weak boundaries, fear of conflict, or exaggerated perfectionism. That way you can see whether the environment is mainly grinding you down or whether you are unconsciously helping to prolong every feedback loop.

name: Director
seo_title: Career & Burnout Test: Director | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Crushed between editorial demands, budget pressure, and egos on set? The C.O.R.E. test analyzes your real blockages as a director. Free.
infobox: The reality as a Director: You carry the overall creative responsibility, but rarely the full decision-making power. Between editorial, production, broadcaster demands, funding logic, and tight shooting days, vision often turns into damage control. You have to lead acting, camera, locations, timing, and team dynamics at the same time, while every bad decision burns money immediately. The pressure is high, recognition is unevenly distributed, and the next green light often depends not just on quality, but on contacts, timing, and political fit.
seo_deepdive_h2: When creative authority meets dependency and constant evaluation
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like "assert yourself more clearly" often falls flat in directing. You work in chains of power where too much resistance quickly gets you labeled as difficult. Many directors compensate for this uncertainty with overcontrol, constant self-optimization, or compliant adaptation to editorial teams and producers. Behind that are often fear of survival, imposter syndrome, and the fear of being shut out of the commissioning system after a weak project. The C.O.R.E. Index shows whether the system is really blocking you or whether unconscious fear of conflict, weak boundaries, and a need for validation are weakening your directing.
faq_q1: How does the test help if I function on set but mentally crash between projects?
faq_a1: That is exactly where the diagnostics start. Many directors perform at a high level during production and only break down in the idle phases between pitches, funding applications, and unclear commitments. The test separates acute production stress from deeper patterns like fear of survival, external validation seeking, and chronic tension caused by insecure project economics. That makes it visible whether your problem lies mainly in the industry, in your self-management, or in both.

name: Screenwriter
seo_title: Career & Burnout Test for Screenwriters | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Worn down by development, notes, and constant uncertainty? The C.O.R.E. test shows what is really blocking you in the writing profession.
infobox: The reality as a Screenwriter: You work for months on material that never gets produced. Between synopsis, treatment, draft, and endless rounds of notes, your creative work is constantly torn apart, often by editorial teams, producers, or funding logic that mainly wants to avoid risk. Payment comes late, in fragments, or only per project. At the same time, you are supposed to write something original and still match market expectations exactly. This mix of precarious planning, constant evaluation, and vague authorship eats away at self-worth, focus, and any clear boundary between work and self-doubt.
seo_deepdive_h2: When every note feels like a judgment on your worth
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like "just handle feedback more professionally" falls short here. For screenwriters, the text is never just a product; it is often tightly linked to identity, the need for recognition, and survival. That is exactly why notes, silence after pitches, or arbitrary project cancellations hit so deeply. Many stay too long in bad projects because scarcity fear makes them cling to every assignment. Others get stuck in perfectionism to avoid rejection. The C.O.R.E. Index separates systemic market problems from unconscious patterns like imposter syndrome, conflict avoidance, and weak boundaries.
faq_q1: Will the test help me if I doubt my talent after every round of notes?
faq_a1: Yes. The test does not judge your artistic quality, but your psychological reaction to a market where criticism, non-commitment, and power imbalances are structurally built in. It shows whether external industry mechanisms are mainly grinding you down or whether inner patterns like perfectionism, the need for recognition, or fear of survival make you process feedback as personal proof of inadequacy.

name: PR Manager
seo_title: Burnout & Career Test for PR Managers | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Constant crisis, approval loops, reputation risk? The C.O.R.E. test shows whether system pressure, fear of conflict, or chronic tension is blocking you.
infobox: The reality as a PR Manager: You work in a permanent state of latent crisis readiness: press inquiries with tight deadlines, internal approval loops, nervous executives, and the constant fear of reputational damage. Externally everything is supposed to look controlled, while internally there is often politics, silo thinking, and contradictory messages. You sell credibility even though facts arrive too late, filtered, or not at all. On top of that comes the expectation of constant availability, because one careless statement, one shitstorm, or one badly briefed spokesperson can trigger days of damage control.
seo_deepdive_h2: When control is your job, but never really possible
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like "prioritize better" often does not work in PR, because your stress comes not only from tasks, but from reputational liability, power imbalances, and constant outside judgment. You are the buffer zone between media logic and internal sugarcoating. Many PR managers get stuck in overadaptation because fear of conflict, the need for recognition, or survival anxiety stops them from clearly naming bad briefings, unrealistic statements, or toxic approval chains. The C.O.R.E. Index separates it cleanly: what is structural organizational failure, and what is your unconscious tendency to take on responsibility that is not actually yours?
faq_q1: What good is the test if my main problem is chaotic approvals and political communication inside the company?
faq_a1: That is exactly where the evaluation starts. It does not just assess your stress level, but the cause: whether dysfunctional stakeholder structures, unclear decision-making, and constant reputational risk are grinding you down, or whether your own patterns like conflict avoidance, people-pleasing, and exaggerated responsibility-taking are making things worse. That matters, because the solution is completely different depending on the cause.

name: Translator
seo_title: Career & Burnout Test for Translators | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Price pressure, deadlines, invisible work? C.O.R.E. shows whether fear of survival, perfectionism, or weak boundaries are wearing you down in translation.
infobox: The reality as a Translator: You work in a market where linguistic precision is expected but rarely paid properly. Deadlines are tight, briefs are incomplete, and in the end almost nobody sees how much terminology work, research, and coordination actually goes into a clean target text. Then there is brutal price pressure, competition from machine translation, and clients who want quality but negotiate word rates like it is mass-produced junk. Anyone who survives here is not just fighting with texts, but constantly with invisibility, self-doubt, and fear of the next dry spell.
seo_deepdive_h2: When perfectionism and price pressure work against each other
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like "position yourself better" or "enforce higher rates" falls short. As a translator, you often get stuck between professional conscience, tight delivery dates, and clients who treat linguistic nuance as interchangeable. That is where unconscious blockages arise: fear of survival makes you accept bad conditions, perfectionism eats your margin, and weak boundaries turn every follow-up question into free proofreading. The C.O.R.E. Index shows whether the market is really grinding you down or whether inner patterns like conflict avoidance, imposter syndrome, and chronic submission to client pressure are systematically making your professional life worse.
faq_q1: How does the test help if I keep accepting too-low word rates and doing revisions for free?
faq_a1: It separates market problems from behavior patterns. Low fees are real in many language pairs, but not everyone reacts the same way. The evaluation checks whether fear of survival, the need for recognition, conflict avoidance, or an exaggerated quality standard are causing you to fail at holding boundaries, charging for extra work, and consistently underselling yourself in negotiations.

name: Editor
seo_title: Burnout & Career Test for Editors | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Crushed between deadlines, author egos, and cost-cutting? The C.O.R.E. test shows what is really blocking you in editing.
infobox: The reality as an Editor: You work in a constant barrage of manuscript piles, tight production schedules, and contradictory expectations from publishers, authors, and sales. On the one hand, you are supposed to be linguistically precise, stylistically sensitive, and substantively strict; on the other, fast, low-conflict, and economical. Especially in fiction or non-fiction editing, the permanent responsibility for details drains energy: errors must not slip through, but real care is often neither properly budgeted nor given enough time. Then there are precarious fees, invisible work, and the constant pressure to phrase criticism in a way that improves the text without setting fragile egos off.
seo_deepdive_h2: When precision, harmony addiction, and precarity collide
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like "prioritize better" often does not work in editing. You are not only responsible for language, but for quality under conditions that systematically undermine quality. Many editors get stuck in a mix of perfectionism, conflict avoidance, and fear of survival: you smooth one more round, quietly take on extra work, and avoid clear boundaries so you do not lose authors, publishers, or clients. That is where the wear and tear comes from. The C.O.R.E. Index separates cleanly what is structural exploitation and what is being amplified inside you by imposter syndrome, the helper role, or weak boundaries.
faq_q1: How do I deal with authors who read my editing as a personal attack?
faq_a1: That is a core conflict of the job. Proper editing almost always touches the author side's identity, vanity, and need for control. The key is to strictly separate text criticism from the person, explain interventions clearly, and name the conflict openly: readability, coherence, market demands, or tone. If you keep softening criticism to avoid tension, either the text quality suffers or your psychological strain rises. Professional boundaries here are not harshness; they are a requirement for doing good work.

name: Motion Designer
seo_title: Burnout Test for Motion Designers | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Tired of endless feedback loops and pixel ping-pong? The C.O.R.E. test shows whether perfectionism, price pressure, or chaos is blocking you.
infobox: The reality as a Motion Designer: You work at the intersection of creativity, technology, and client politics - and that is exactly where it gets exhausting. Endless feedback loops, vague briefs, spontaneous scope changes, and unrealistic deadlines are the norm. On top of that comes the pressure to deliver something 'fresh' all the time, even though budgets are tight and timelines are absurd. Anyone who brings After Effects, Premiere, 3D tools, and a feel for sound quickly gets turned into a jack-of-all-trades. Your own quality standards constantly collide with approval processes, micromanagement, and clients who want motion but have no clear idea.
seo_deepdive_h2: When perfectionism meets price pressure and constant feedback
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like 'communicate more clearly' or 'set boundaries' often falls short in this profession. As a Motion Designer, you are not just tied to your timeline, but to hierarchies, approvals, and taste judgments that are often technically weak. Many stay too long in bad projects because fear of survival, imposter syndrome, or the urge to prove themselves through strong work sabotages any boundary-setting. The C.O.R.E. Index separates it cleanly: is your stress mainly caused by a system of chaos, dumping prices, and revisions - or by unconscious patterns that drive you into endless self-overload?
faq_q1: Does the test help if constant revisions and unclear briefs are what are really wearing me out?
faq_a1: Yes. Those kinds of burdens are central: vague client wishes, the tenth round of changes, missing creative direction, and the pressure to still deliver fast. The evaluation shows whether dysfunctional project structures are mainly exhausting you or whether conflict avoidance, perfectionism, and weak pricing or boundary-setting are making the problem worse.

name: Game Designer
seo_title: Burnout & Career Test for Game Designers | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Crushed between crunch, feature creep, and feedback loops? The C.O.R.E. test shows what is really blocking you in game design.
infobox: The reality as a Game Designer: You work at the intersection of creativity, production, and monetization - and that is exactly where it gets ugly. Ideas die in scope meetings, features get watered down by stakeholders, and in the end you are still supposed to deliver a coherent core loop, solid balancing, and good retention. Then there are crunch phases, constant iteration, contradictory feedback from leads, PMs, and the community, plus the pressure to justify every patch with metrics. Many game designers get stuck between vision and Jira tickets.
seo_deepdive_h2: When creative identity collides with production logic
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like "prioritize better" often falls short in game design. The problem is not just organization, but a permanent conflict between creative ambition, data logic, and hierarchy. Anyone who defines themselves strongly through ideas, systems, and player experience quickly experiences every cut as a personal loss of value. At the same time, fear of survival, imposter syndrome, and weak boundaries keep many in projects that are already grinding them down internally. The C.O.R.E. Index separates it cleanly: what is a dysfunctional studio system - and what is your unconscious tendency to compensate for bad processes?
faq_q1: What if I constantly have to defend features that later get cut anyway because of scope or monetization?
faq_a1: Then your problem is very likely not a lack of resilience, but a structural role conflict. Game designers often carry responsibility for player experience and system coherence, but have no real decision-making power over budget, roadmap, or live ops priorities. That creates constant frustration and self-doubt. The diagnostics help you see whether you are working in a studio with chronically sloppy product management or whether conflict avoidance and over-identification with your concepts are pulling you deeper into that loop.

name: Radio presenter
seo_title: Career & stress test for radio presenters | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Constant tension on air, ratings pressure, and social pressure? The C.O.R.E. test shows whether system stress or inner blocks are wearing you down.
infobox: The reality as a radio presenter: You sound relaxed even though timing, ratings pressure, and constant self-monitoring are running behind the microphone. Every segment has to land exactly right, every slip of the tongue is instantly audible, and every weakness can become a topic in the newsroom. Add early shifts, wrecked biorhythms, poor predictability, and the expectation to seem spontaneous, approachable, and broadcast-ready even on bad days. The industry runs on replaceability: format changes, budget cuts, and fixed-term contracts make it clear how quickly a voice can be swapped out.
seo_deepdive_h2: When your voice becomes the brand and you burn out inside
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like 'just be authentic' falls short in radio. You work in a system that simulates closeness but punishes mistakes hard. Many presenters therefore develop constant self-monitoring: voice, timing, impact, social feedback, newsroom climate. Behind that are often existential fear, strong dependence on external validation, and the fear of losing relevance without an on-air role. The C.O.R.E. Index cleanly separates whether a draining station environment is wearing you down or whether unconscious adaptation patterns are keeping you stuck in that strain.
faq_q1: Does the test help even if I function on air but feel massive pressure and fear of failure before every show?
faq_a1: Yes. That contradiction is common in radio: confident on the outside, in alarm mode on the inside. The evaluation does not measure your presenting quality, but the psychological patterns behind performance pressure, perfectionism, and dependence on listener or newsroom feedback. That makes it visible whether this is mainly normal tension before live situations or a chronic stress pattern that can turn into exhaustion, cynicism, or self-alienation over time.

name: Publishing clerk
seo_title: Career test for publishing clerks | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Caught between program pressure, tight budgets, and constant coordination? The C.O.R.E. test shows what is really blocking you in publishing.
infobox: The reality as a publishing clerk: You hold together a system that looks cultural on the outside but is often driven internally by margin pressure, tight advances, narrow publication deadlines, and constant coordination. Between editorial, production, sales, rights management, and marketing, you are the interface for everything that can go wrong. Errors in budgeting, metadata, print run planning, or delivery quickly land on you. On top of that, you work in a market where idealism is gladly exploited: lots of responsibility, little visibility, rarely any real planning security.
seo_deepdive_h2: When cultural ambition meets permanent economic pressure
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like "prioritize better" often falls short in publishing because your day is not made up of clear tasks, but of conflicting interests. You are supposed to maintain author relationships, keep costs in view, schedule programs cleanly, and absorb every bit of friction internally. That is exactly where unconscious patterns kick in: fear of conflict prevents clear boundaries, perfectionism eats time, and existential fear keeps you in structures that live off underpayment and quiet self-exploitation. The C.O.R.E. Index separates personal blocks from a system that happily uses cultural passion against you.
faq_q1: What does the test do for me if my main problem is the constant chaos between program planning, production, and sales?
faq_a1: It shows whether you are mainly suffering from dysfunctional publishing structures or whether your own patterns are making the chaos worse. For example, you may be taking on too much buffer work, avoiding escalation too late, or compensating for bad processes with over-responsibility. That distinction is crucial if you want to do more than manage symptoms and actually make solid career decisions.

name: Brand manager
seo_title: Career check for brand managers | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Crushed between brand strategy, sales pressure, and internal politics? C.O.R.E. shows what really blocks you as a brand manager.
infobox: The reality as a brand manager: You are supposed to lead a brand consistently while sales, product, management, and agencies constantly interfere with positioning. On one hand, strategic clarity is demanded; on the other, quarterly targets, discount campaigns, and internal sensitivities decide the direction. You are responsible for brand image, launches, and campaigns, often without real decision-making power. When KPIs slip, the brand quickly becomes the scapegoat. Add endless coordination, fragile stakeholder egos, and constant pressure to squeeze creativity into politically safe presentations.
seo_deepdive_h2: The core conflict: brand responsibility without real control
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like "prioritize better" or "communicate more clearly" falls short here. As a brand manager, you are rarely stuck because of a lack of discipline, but because of contradictory power structures. You are supposed to show backbone, but you are not allowed to offend anyone internally. That is exactly where unconscious patterns act: fear of conflict, a strong need for approval, or imposter syndrome lead you to accept bad briefs, go through too many loops, and water down your own line. C.O.R.E. cleanly separates whether your problem lies in the system or in psychological patterns that keep you stuck in politically charged brand roles.
faq_q1: What if I know the brand strategy, but sales or management keep watering it down internally?
faq_a1: That is a typical pattern in brand roles. Then the problem is not missing marketing knowledge, but a structural conflict between brand leadership, short-term revenue pressure, and internal power distribution. The assessment checks whether you are being blocked by the facts in such situations or whether fear of friction, a need for harmony, or dependence on status makes you give in too early and lose your own profile in the process.

name: Content creator
seo_title: Burnout test for content creators | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Worn out by posting pressure, platform moods, and constant comparison? The C.O.R.E. test shows what really blocks you as a content creator.
infobox: The reality as a content creator: You produce under constant output pressure for algorithms that can turn against you at any time. Reach drops without warning, even though you are editing, writing, filming, and posting more than ever. At the same time, you are supposed to seem authentic, market yourself, spot trends early, and take criticism publicly. The boundaries between work, private life, and self-staging disappear completely. Anyone depending on views, partnerships, or client inquiries often lives in a mix of creative exhaustion, income insecurity, and constant comparison with seemingly more successful accounts.
seo_deepdive_h2: When your self-worth depends on reach and reactions
seo_deepdive_text: Standard tips like "post more consistently" or "just be yourself" fall short because they ignore the core problem: as a content creator, you are not only the producer, but often the product itself. Every weak performance can feel like personal failure. That creates over-adaptation, constant availability, and the fear that taking a break will make you invisible to the algorithm. The C.O.R.E. Index checks whether existential fear, imposter syndrome, weak boundaries, or addiction to external validation are keeping you trapped in an exhausting loop.
faq_q1: How does the test distinguish normal creator stress from real psychological overload caused by algorithm pressure?
faq_a1: It does not just assess workload, but the patterns behind it: compulsion to be constantly present, fear about income, dependence on likes, lack of separation between person and brand, and reactions to reach losses. That makes it visible whether you simply have an intense workload or are already stuck in a structurally risky mix of self-exploitation, comparison pressure, and emotional over-identification with your performance.

name: Illustrator
seo_title: Career & burnout test: Illustrator | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Worn down by revision loops, dumping prices, and endless pitches? The C.O.R.E. test shows what really blocks you as an illustrator.
infobox: The reality as an illustrator: You are not just selling image ideas, but constantly selling your right to be paid for them. Between badly briefed clients, endless revision loops, buyout discussions, and price dumping through platforms, there is often barely any room for focused work. On top of that comes the pressure to be both distinctive and endlessly adaptable. Visibility on social media drains energy, unpaid pitching drains time, and many jobs fail not because of quality, but because of weak contracts and poor boundaries.
seo_deepdive_h2: When creative identity meets price pressure and blurred boundaries
seo_deepdive_text: Illustrators are often told they just need to negotiate better or post more. That is too shallow. If you live off commissions, you quickly fall into a psychological trap: existential fear makes you pliable, the need for recognition makes you revision-prone, and imposter syndrome prevents clear fee demands. Because your work is so tightly tied to your identity, every critique quickly feels personal. The C.O.R.E. Index cleanly separates an objectively exploitative market environment from the inner patterns that push you into bad briefs, free samples, and chronic self-exploitation.
faq_q1: Does the test help me even if my main problem is constant revision loops and unclear briefs?
faq_a1: Yes. That is often exactly where it becomes clear whether the problem is only the client or whether weak boundaries, conflict avoidance, and fear of losing the job are making the situation worse. The evaluation shows why you stop too late, think too much for free, or accept unclear approvals. That way you can see whether you mainly have a contract problem, a communication problem, or a psychological pattern problem.

name: Comic artist
seo_title: Career test for comic artists | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Deadlines, page rates, visibility pressure? The C.O.R.E. test shows whether market logic, self-doubt, or weak boundaries are blocking you.
infobox: The reality as a comic artist: You work on pages, panels, storyboards, and characters, often alone for weeks, under massive time pressure and uncertain pay. Between commissioned work, personal projects, social media presence, and revision loops, there is hardly any predictable recovery time. Many publishers pay poorly, many clients want usage rights thrown in cheaply, and in freelance life, pitching eats time that should really go into drawing. Constant comparison with more stylistically strong or more productive colleagues eats away at self-worth, while creative blocks immediately feel existential.
seo_deepdive_h2: When creativity becomes permanent evaluation
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like "post more" or "be more disciplined" falls short. As a comic artist, you are not only selling output, but handwriting, style, and visual identity. That is exactly why rejection hits harder than in many other jobs. Many stay in badly paid projects because they fear being seen as difficult or losing the next job. Add perfectionism, imposter syndrome, and weak boundaries around revisions. The C.O.R.E. Index makes visible whether the market is really blocking you or whether unconscious existential fear is keeping you in self-exploitation.
faq_q1: How do I deal with a client who still demands new changes without extra pay after the third revision round?
faq_a1: That is not an exception, but a classic boundary problem in creative project relationships. The professionally clean approach is to define revision rounds, usage rights, page rate, and scope of changes in writing beforehand. Psychologically, this often fails not because of a lack of knowledge, but because of fear of conflict and the worry of seeming uncooperative. That is exactly where the assessment comes in: it shows whether you are giving in because of real market dependence or because of a pattern that systematically devalues your work.

name: Voice actor
seo_title: Career & burnout test for voice actors | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Vocally in demand, economically under pressure? The C.O.R.E. test shows whether market logic, price pressure, and self-doubt are blocking you.
infobox: The reality as a voice actor: You work in an industry where few roles are visible, but many people compete for the same jobs. Castings, takes, retakes, and tight directing notes are part of everyday life. Add fee pressure, buyouts, fluctuating workload, and the constant need to deliver vocally on point, even when you are already exhausted inside. Your voice is your tool and your risk at the same time: an infection, hoarseness, or psychological stress can directly cost you income.
seo_deepdive_h2: When artistic identity meets price pressure and replaceability
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like "network better" or "state your price confidently" often falls short in voice acting. You operate in a market with heavy competition, informal access, and strong dependence on studios, scheduling, and direction. Many voice actors stay in bad conditions because existential fear, the need to be seen as easy to work with, and hidden self-doubt undermine every clear boundary. C.O.R.E. analyzes whether the market is really slowing you down, or whether weak boundaries and fear of the next quiet month are steering your decisions.
faq_q1: How does the test help me if I cave in during fee negotiations despite good takes?
faq_a1: The test does not assess your vocal quality, but the psychological patterns behind your negotiation behavior. It shows whether you systematically undersell yourself out of fear of losing the job, pressure to adapt, or a hidden imposter syndrome. That is crucial in voice acting, because good performance alone does not create a stable position.

name: Podcast producer
seo_title: Burnout test for podcast producers | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Deadline pressure, bad briefs, endless revision loops? The C.O.R.E. test shows whether system stress or weak boundaries are wearing you down.
infobox: The reality as a podcast producer: You hold formats together technically and editorially while everyone else only hears the finished audio. The day-to-day is made up of chaotic briefs, tight budgets, remote recordings with bad sound, last-minute changes, and revision loops that are never properly paid. Between editing, sound design, publishing, host support, and marketing pressure, you are often producer, firefighter, and punching bag all at once. infobox: The reality as a water resources engineer: You work on critical infrastructure, but often under conditions that are barely solvable in a clean professional way. Between Water Framework Directive requirements, flood protection, watercourse maintenance, contaminated sites, low water levels, and political budget limits, you are supposed to minimize risks even though the data is incomplete and responsibilities are fragmented. You feel personally liable for every extreme event while municipalities save money, residents protest, and decisions are slowed down by procurement, planning approval, and permitting processes.
seo_deepdive_h2: When professional responsibility meets political short-sightedness
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like "prioritize better" falls short here because your everyday reality is not just workload, but contradictory system logic. You are supposed to prepare for problems even though budgets only loosen after damage has already happened. Many water resources engineers compensate with excessive control, constant availability, or silent self-overload. Behind that are often existential fear around wrong decisions, a technical perfection pattern, and an inability to clearly limit professionally questionable requirements. The C.O.R.E. Index cuts through the noise: what is a real structural flaw in the system, and what is your psychological attachment to responsibility that has long become unhealthy?
faq_q1: What does the test give me if my main problem is not the technical work, but the pressure between authority, politics, and riparian landowners?
faq_a1: That is exactly where the diagnosis starts. It does not only assess workload, but also role conflicts, diffusion of responsibility, conflict avoidance, and the psychological burden of liability and safety issues. That makes it visible whether dysfunctional administrative structures are wearing you down, or whether inner patterns like over-responsibility, harmony-seeking, or fear of escalation are causing you to carry far too much.

name: Horticultural Engineer
seo_title: Burnout & Career Test for Horticultural Engineers | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Crushed between weather, cost pressure, and regulations? The C.O.R.E. test shows whether system stress or inner blocks are keeping you stuck in horticulture.
infobox: The reality as a horticultural engineer: You work at the intersection of biology, technology, business, and administrative logic. In practice that means weather risk, pest pressure, tight margins, seasonal peaks, and constant proof of quality, yield, and sustainability. At the same time, farms, municipalities, or clients expect reliable solutions even though staff are missing, budgets are being cut, and every wrong decision becomes visible on the ground. The pressure is high because you are supposed to control natural processes that do not fit neatly into Excel.
seo_deepdive_h2: When nature, costs, and responsibility all pull on you at once
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like prioritizing better or appearing more confident often falls short in horticulture. You cannot organize away weather extremes, disease pressure, or volatile markets. Many horticultural engineers compensate for this uncertainty with control, constant availability, and an exaggerated sense of responsibility. That is where exhaustion begins. Anyone who keeps taking crop failures, complaints, or miscalculations personally will quickly end up in a mix of existential fear, perfectionism, and silent self-overload. The C.O.R.E. Index separates the two cleanly: what is systemic pressure, and what is your unconscious attachment to an environment that is draining you dry?
faq_q1: How do I deal with being blamed for yield problems as a horticultural engineer when weather, disease, and budget limits are the main causes?
faq_a1: First: responsibility and blame are not the same thing. In horticultural systems, site conditions, weather, variety choice, plant protection, substrate, irrigation, staff quality, and purchasing decisions all act at the same time. The only serious approach is therefore a root-cause analysis with solid data: crop management, assessments, climate data, nutrient supply, failure rates, and documentation of measures. Psychologically, the important part is setting boundaries against blanket blame. If you are permanently carrying a farm’s structural risks alone, chronic stress develops. Then not only the technical strategy needs review, but also whether roles, liability, and expectation management in the business are fundamentally wrong.

faq_q1: I work cleanly, I’m strong technically, and yet I’m constantly exhausted. What is it really often about in the chemistry world?

In lab and research environments, a small methodological error can cost days, while publication pressure, deadlines, and GMP or safety requirements keep running. This kind of constant strain often leads to inner alarm, excessive self-control, and the feeling of never really being done.

name: Meteorologist

seo_desc: Caught between forecast pressure, shift work, and fear of false alarms? The C.O.R.E. test shows what really wears you down in meteorology.
infobox: The reality as a meteorologist: You work in a field where high technical precision is expected, even though the atmosphere remains a chaotic system. Whether it's forecasting, severe weather situations, or aviation weather: every forecast is under scrutiny, and mistakes are publicly visible. Shift work, night shifts, and constant model comparisons grind down concentration and your biological rhythm. On top of that comes the pressure to communicate complex uncertainty in a way that laypeople, media, or emergency services can turn into clear decisions.

seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like "just prioritize better" falls short here. As a meteorologist, you carry responsibility in a system that can never be fully controlled. Index checks whether you're blocked by genuine overload or whether imposter syndrome, conflict avoidance, and an inability to tolerate residual uncertainty are slowly undermining your career and health.
faq_q1: How do I deal with it when I issue a severe weather warning despite clean data, and it later turns out to have been a false alarm?
faq_a1: Professionally, a warning decision should not be judged by the later outcome alone, but by the quality of the situation assessment at the time of the decision. In meteorology, you work with probabilities, ensemble spread, nowcasting signals, and limited predictability. Psychologically, it becomes critical when you treat every false alarm as personal proof of incompetence against yourself. That creates defensive forecasting: warning too cautiously to avoid criticism. That shift can be more dangerous than a well-founded false alarm.

name: Statistician
seo_title: Career & burnout test: statisticians | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Crushed between significance, time pressure, and expectations outside your field? The C.O.R.E. test analyzes your blockages as a statistician. Free.
infobox: The reality as a statistician: You're supposed to pull reliable conclusions out of dirty data, often under time pressure and with stakeholders who only accept p-values, confidence intervals, or model assumptions if the result matches what they expected. Instead of clean methodology, deadlines, publication pressure, questionable causal interpretations, and the demand for 'significant' results dominate. You are professionally accountable for uncertainty while others present your analyses in a stripped-down way. Constant error avoidance, reproducibility demands, and the fear of being methodologically attacked create a permanent baseline tension.
seo_deepdive_h2: When methodological integrity meets expectation pressure
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like 'communicate more clearly' falls short when the environment does not want to hear uncertainty. As a statistician, you often sit between science, management, and specialist departments that demand clear answers even though the data cannot provide them. That's where unconscious patterns kick in: conflict avoidance leads to watered-down recommendations, imposter syndrome to endless extra analyses, and existential fear to staying in professionally dubious projects. The C.O.R.E. Index separates systemic pressure from your inner drivers and shows why you remain stuck in constant tension despite high competence.
faq_q1: What if I'm constantly asked to explain results in a 'simpler' way and methodological precision gets lost?
faq_a1: That's a classic role conflict. Good statistics requires simplification in communication, but not distortion of uncertainty, effect sizes, or model limits. If you're regularly pushed to leave out limitations or sell correlations as causation, that's not just a communication problem, it's a structural risk. The assessment helps you see whether external bad incentives are mainly wearing you down, or whether conflict avoidance and exaggerated perfectionism are stopping you from setting clear professional boundaries.

name: Historian
seo_title: Career & strain test for historians | C.O.R.E.

infobox: The reality as a historian: Your everyday life rarely consists of quiet scholarship. Between archive work, source criticism, footnotes, and committee logic, there is little room for real intellectual depth. Anyone who wants to stay in academia often has to accept geographic mobility, uncertain prospects, and years of self-discipline. At the same time, competition for positions is fierce, while outside the university the question keeps coming up: what is all this actually worth, professionally and financially?

seo_deepdive_text: Historians are often told to network more strategically or move into non-academic fields earlier. That misses the point. If you've invested years in research, editions, and teaching, you often tie your self-worth to academic recognition. That's exactly what makes fixed-term dependence so effective. It's not just the job market blocking you, but also fear of loss, perfectionism, and the feeling that without a professorship you've failed. The C.O.R.E. Index cleanly separates real structural problems in the academic system from the inner patterns that keep you waiting in academia's holding room despite exhaustion.
faq_q1: Will the test help me if I'm stuck between a postdoc, a third-party funded project, and leaving the university?
faq_a1: Yes. That's exactly where a sober diagnosis makes sense. The test does not evaluate your publication list or academic excellence; it analyzes whether your attachment to the university is driven mainly by real career options, fear of status loss, academic identity, or a lack of boundaries toward a precarious system. That creates a solid basis for deciding between staying, strategic repositioning, or a deliberate exit.

name: Archaeologist

Index separates things cleanly: what is a structural dead end, and where are loyalty, self-worth doubts, or poor boundaries artificially keeping you stuck?
faq_q1: Will the test also help me if I'm stuck between an excavation project, a museum, and an academic career?
faq_a1: Yes. That's exactly where it's useful. The assessment doesn't just look at general dissatisfaction, but at the psychological patterns behind your decision paralysis: is real lack of prospects in the academic system holding you back, or is hope for the next position keeping you tied to chronic uncertainty? name: Sociologist

Index separates things cleanly: is your exhaustion mainly caused by the academic system - or by unconscious patterns like conflict avoidance, over-adaptation, and chronic self-devaluation?

name: Geographer

seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like 'position yourself more clearly' often falls short for geographers. A serious diagnosis therefore separates systemic causes - such as poor role design, project logic, and fixed-term contracts - from your individual pattern, such as weak boundaries, conflict avoidance, or the urge to constantly prove your worth.

name: Materials Scientist

Publication pressure, dependence on third-party funding, and slow approval processes mean you're ground down between scientific care and utilitarian logic.

seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like 'prioritize better' or 'communicate more clearly' falls short here. As a materials scientist, you're tied to measurement devices, lab windows, approvals, and hypotheses that cannot be controlled by calendar. Many stay too long in dysfunctional projects because they interpret negative results as personal failure instead of part of real research. That's where imposter syndrome, conflict avoidance, and fear of survival come in: you keep pushing clear boundaries back, endlessly rework datasets, and hope the next attempt will finally legitimize your career. The C.O.R.E. Index separates systemic research barriers from your unconscious patterns.
faq_q1: What does the test give me if my main problem isn't overload, but constantly inconsistent measurements and project uncertainty?
faq_a1: That's exactly what's relevant. The test doesn't just assess workload, but also your psychological reaction to uncertainty, reproducibility problems, and external dependencies like instrument availability, reviewer logic, or PI pressure. name: Geneticist

Between the lab bench, bioinformatics pipelines, ethics requirements, and PI expectations, there is often little room for real intellectual calm.

seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like 'prioritize better' often doesn't work in genetics. You can do everything cleanly and still crash into Reviewer 2, failed replications, or expiring funding. That's where hidden patterns emerge: imposter syndrome with highly complex methods, weak boundaries toward PIs, fear of survival through serial fixed-term contracts, and exaggerated responsibility pressure around clinical findings. The C.O.R.E. Index soberly separates what is structurally broken from what internally keeps you from setting boundaries, changing tracks, or realistically weighing responsibility.

faq_a1: Yes. Those transitions are psychologically tricky because they don't just mean different tasks, but different forms of responsibility, visibility, and consequences of mistakes. The assessment looks not only at stress level, but also at whether status loss, need for security, perfectionism, or fear of professional devaluation is keeping you in a role that doesn't fit.

name: Science Journalist

seo_deepdive_text: Standard career tips like 'position yourself more clearly' or 'sell yourself better' fall short here. In science journalism, it's not just the market that blocks you, but often an inner conflict: if you work carefully, you doubt longer, check more, and publish later. That clashes directly with editorial logic and click economics. Add existential fear in freelance work, imposter syndrome toward researchers, and weak boundaries around socially charged topics. C.O.R.E. soberly separates what is structural precarity from what keeps you stuck psychologically in over-adaptation, perfectionism, or constant tension.
faq_q1: What if I'm constantly torn between scientific care and editorial pressure to sharpen the angle?
faq_a1: Then the problem is often not lack of resilience, but a real role conflict. Science journalists have to represent uncertainty correctly, while editors often demand clear theses, strong headlines, and quick usability. That easily leads to self-doubt, rumination loops, and overly long research phases. A solid diagnosis helps distinguish whether you're mainly suffering from dysfunctional market conditions or whether perfectionism, conflict avoidance, and fear of professional exposure are additionally limiting your ability to act.

name: Police Officer
seo_title: Burnout & Career Test for Police Officers | C.O.R.E.

Between patrol duty, domestic violence, traffic accidents, and criminal complaints, there is often barely any room to process what you have experienced properly. At the same time, the system demands maximum professionalism with minimal tolerance for mistakes: one wrong move, one stupid sentence, or incomplete documentation can have disciplinary consequences. seo_deepdive_text: Police officers are often told they just need to become more resilient or switch off better in private. That misses the point. Anyone who regularly experiences threats, aggression, and extreme human situations often develops emotional shutdown as a survival strategy. It becomes a problem when exactly that hardness blocks any honest self-awareness. Add to that corps spirit, fear of weakness, and the worry of being seen as unfit for duty. The C.O.R.E. Index analyzes whether it is mainly systemic pressure grinding you down, or whether unconscious existential fear, poor boundaries, and an exaggerated sense of duty are keeping you stuck in the strain.

faq_a1: That is exactly where the analysis makes sense. Many police officers do not break down on duty, but show the consequences later: irritability, sleep problems, withdrawal, sarcasm, or emotional distance in the family. The test does not check your operational toughness; it checks the psychological patterns behind that functioning mode. It helps distinguish whether you are reacting to acute overload, creeping numbness, unresolved operational experiences, or chronic boundary violations. seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like "take better care of yourself" often falls flat in the fire service. You work in a system where reliability comes first and absences hit the team immediately. Unprocessed operational images, helper syndrome, guilt after mistakes, and fear of failing at the critical moment block clear decisions. The C.O.R.E. Index cuts through it bluntly: is it the station structure, or are your own inner patterns what is keeping you stuck?

faq_a1: Yes. That pattern is common in fire service work: controlled on scene, then internally overclocked, irritable, or emotionally cut off at home. The test does not replace trauma diagnostics, but it precisely captures whether your state is more about chronic overload, weak boundaries, suppressed processing of incidents, or a risky adaptation to station culture. name: Soldier

seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like "take better care of yourself" falls short in a military context. faq_a1: That distinction is exactly what is hard in a military environment, because suppression is often mistaken for functioning. The key is not only open breakdowns, but early indicators: emotional numbness, irritability, sleep problems, social withdrawal, cynicism, declining concentration, and the feeling of running on autopilot. faq_a1: That is not a side issue in investigative work; it is a typical strain pattern. A solid assessment helps distinguish between professionally normal uncertainty and psychological over-identification with the case. name: Prison Officer

name: Lifeguard

Shift work, heat, noise, aggressive visitors, staff shortages, and monotonous vigilance alternate with adrenaline spikes. The hardest part is not only the rescue itself, but the constant tension before it: being ready at any moment when others become careless.
seo_deepdive_h2: The core conflict: constant vigilance without real relief
seo_deepdive_text: Standard tips like 'switch off better' or 'set boundaries' fall short here. As a lifeguard, you cannot afford mental downtime, because seconds decide between life, brain damage, or a false alarm. That permanent state of alert eats energy. Many also stay trapped in an unhealthy functioning mode: helper syndrome, fear of fatal mistakes, exaggerated responsibility, and guilt after incidents. The C.O.R.E. Index cleanly separates what in your condition comes from real system pressure and what is fed by unconscious patterns like weak boundaries, conflict avoidance, or latent existential fear.
faq_q1: What good is the test if the monotony of pool supervision and the fear of missing an emergency are what wear me down?
faq_a1: That is exactly where the assessment starts. It does not only evaluate acute operational strain, but also the psychological burden from vigilance, sensory overload, liability pressure, and the constant fear of missing that one critical moment. That way you can see whether your exhaustion comes mainly from the working conditions or whether inner patterns like exaggerated responsibility, perfectionism, or weak boundaries are making the pressure worse.

name: Administrative Officer

seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like "prioritize better" often falls flat in administration because your room to maneuver is limited by law, responsibility, appeal risks, and hierarchy. Index cleanly separates what is real system overload and where control compulsion, conflict avoidance, or weak boundaries are additionally blocking you.

name: Diplomat

seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like "set better boundaries" falls short in diplomatic service. In your daily life, emotional restraint is not a lifestyle choice; it is a job requirement. Anyone who constantly has to project composure often notices too late how strongly inner tension, status anxiety, and loyalty conflicts are already working. Add hidden patterns: fear of being internationally burned by one misstep, or imposter syndrome in highly selective elite networks. C.O.R.E. cleanly separates what is structural pressure and what comes from unconscious conflict avoidance or excessive adaptation.

faq_a1: Yes. That loyalty conflict is central in diplomatic service. The evaluation does not examine your political stance; it examines your psychological reaction to role tension, hierarchy pressure, constant self-control, and hidden competition in the ministry, embassy, or multilateral mission. name: Civil registrar
seo_title: Stress test for civil registrars | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Caught between civil status law, deadlines, and citizen pressure? The C.O.R.E. test shows whether system overload or inner blockages are grinding you down.
infobox: The reality as a civil registrar: You work at interfaces where mistakes are not embarrassing, but legally relevant: births, marriages, deaths, name law, register corrections. Citizens often come in emotionally charged, expect speed, and confuse service with a legal entitlement. At the same time, civil status law ties you to formalities, deadlines, and proof obligations that leave no room to maneuver. The pressure does not come from volume, but from the constant need to stay correct, neutral, and legally solid, even though every formal error has consequences.
seo_deepdive_h2: The core conflict: showing empathy without becoming legally vulnerable
seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like 'stay calm' or 'set clear boundaries' falls short here. As a civil registrar, you carry sovereign responsibility in highly sensitive life situations. That creates a quiet, constant pressure: you want to treat people fairly, but you cannot bend rules out of pity or wave through unclear documents just to avoid conflict. Many stay in chronic tension because conscientiousness turns into perfectionism, or because fear of complaints, supervisory review, and the consequences of mistakes mentally overloads every decision. The C.O.R.E. Index separates system stress from your unconscious patterns.
faq_q1: What can I do when citizens insist on immediate registration, but the documents are not sufficient under the PStG or international proof law?
faq_a1: The key is a clean separation between communication and legal application. Explain concretely which documents are missing, on what legal basis you need them, and why premature registration would be legally impermissible. Document the process in a traceable way, including the evidence submitted and the guidance given. That lowers your personal risk in complaints and prevents you from creating legal uncertainty under social pressure. Friendliness helps, but it does not replace a defensible file record.

name: Intelligence service employee

seo_deepdive_text: Standard advice like 'talk openly about stress' often goes nowhere in this job, because that openness is systemically limited. Index cleanly separates whether real agency structures are grinding you down, or whether exaggerated control needs, loyalty conflicts, and hidden existential fear are keeping you trapped in the system.

faq_a1: That is exactly why the diagnosis matters. It does not need operational details, case files, or classified content. name: Probation officer
seo_title: Burnout and career test for probation officers | C.O.R.E.

Every bad contact can have consequences. At the same time, you are expected to build workable relationships, assess relapse risks, and provide the court with solid reports. seo_deepdive_text: Standard tips like "set better boundaries" fall short here, because your job cannot be neatly split into closeness and distance. You have to build trust without becoming naive, and exercise control without destroying the relationship. That is where hidden blockages emerge: a helper complex, guilt when sanctions are necessary, fear of false predictions, or the constant inner alarm that you may have missed something. The C.O.R.E. Index analyzes whether professional overload is grinding you down or whether unconscious conflicts are causing you to carry too much, escalate too late, or burn out emotionally.
faq_q1: What does the test give me if my main problem is the high caseload and not my personality?
faq_a1: That is exactly the point: the test does not simply individualize structural overload. It separates systemic burdens like caseload, report pressure, lack of therapy slots, or justice bureaucracy from your personal response patterns. That makes it visible whether you are primarily suffering from bad conditions or whether patterns like over-responsibility, conflict avoidance, or poor distance are making the strain worse. name: Occupational safety specialist
seo_title: Burnout test for occupational safety | C.O.R.E.
seo_desc: Crushed between liability, training sessions, and indifference in the company? C.O.R.E. shows whether system pressure or poor boundaries are burning you out.
infobox: The reality as an occupational safety specialist: You carry responsibility for prevention without having real authority. Between risk assessments, training, inspections, and safety committee meetings, you are supposed to reduce risks effectively while managers prioritize deadlines, costs, and production. After accidents, everyone suddenly demands complete documentation; before that, your warnings are often treated as a nuisance. That contradiction grinds you down: high liability exposure, little enforcement power, constant need to justify yourself, and the feeling of only being taken seriously once something has already gone wrong.
seo_deepdive_h2: Responsibility without power: the core conflict in occupational safety
seo_deepdive_text: The standard advice is often: speak more clearly, warn more consistently, document better. That is too shallow. As an occupational safety specialist, you work in a system where safety is officially a priority, but operationally loses against scheduling, cost pressure, and habit. Many end up in a fatal loop of over-responsibility and self-doubt: Did I warn enough, follow up enough, escalate enough? That is where unconscious patterns like conflict fear, a helper complex, or fear of being personally blamed for an incident take hold. C.O.R.E. cleanly separates structural organizational failure from your individual blockages.
faq_q1: What can I do if my risk assessments are formally ticked off but practically ignored in the company?
faq_a1: Then the problem is usually not the quality of the assessment, but missing leadership responsibility and weak accountability. The key is to attach measures to deadlines, responsible persons, and effectiveness checks, and to document deviations in a defensible way. As an occupational safety specialist, you advise; implementation sits with line management. If warnings are repeatedly ignored, you need a clean escalation through management, the works council, the safety committee, or company leadership. The test helps you see whether the structure is mainly blocking you or whether conflict avoidance is stopping you from initiating necessary escalation steps consistently.

name: Actor

name: Curator

If you are permanently working against your own line, cynicism or quiet quitting follows. A solid diagnostic helps distinguish legitimate adaptation from self-damaging overadaptation.

Index cleanly separates professional responsibility from self-damaging overidentification.
